## Prison Admissions
::: {.cell} ::: {.cell-output-display} ```{=html}

Prison Admission Trends

Sentence about change in admissions from 2010 to 2020.



Admission Types

Prison admissions are shaped by two primary groups: new court commitments, or individuals newly incarcerated for a criminal offense, and parole returns and revocations, those who return to prison for failing to meet the conditions of their parole.

In Rhode Island in 2020, new court commitments accounted for 95% of prison admissions, while parole returns and revocations accounted for 5%.


``` ::: :::

## Parole Eligibility
::: {.cell} ::: {.cell-output-display} ```{=html}

How is Parole Eligibility Determined?

An inmate serving a single sentence that is not a life sentence and who is not classified as a habitual criminal is eligible for parole after serving one-third of the term. Inmates serving multiple terms are eligible for parole after serving one-third of the aggregate time to which they were sentenced, except that inmates serving two or more concurrent terms must serve at least one-third of the maximum term they are required to serve. Inmates already serving one sentence, whether in confinement or on parole, who reoffend and are sentenced for a new crime, are not eligible for parole until they have served one-third of the new sentence (and will not receive credit for the time they served under their previous sentence). If an inmate is sentenced for multiple subsequent offenses, parole eligibility is calculated in the same manner as described above for an original sentence involving multiple offenses.


``` ::: ::: {.cell-output-display} ```{=html}

Prison Population Currently Eligible for Parole

Show breakdown of types:
1) New court commitments/1-24.99 years
2) New court commitments/<1 & >= 25 years
3) Parole returns/1-24.99 years
4) Parole returns/<1 & >= 25 years


``` ::: ::: {.cell-output-display} ```{=html}

Focusing on New Court Commitments with
Sentences of 1 to 24.99 Years

For a nuanced analysis, this section narrows the focus to individuals serving sentences of 1 to 24.99 years for new court commitments.

In 2020, there were 631 people who were in prison and currently eligible for parole. This group made up 69% of the prison population in prison for new court commitments with sentences between 1 to 24.99 years.


``` ::: ::: {.cell-output-display} ```{=html}

Demographics

``` ::: ::: ::: {.panel-tabset .tab2} ### Race and Ethnicity ::: {.cell} ::: {.cell-output-display} ```{=html}

In 2020, among the prison population currently eligible for parole, White, non-Hispanic individuals constituted the majority, representing 37 percent.


``` ::: ::: ### Gender ::: {.cell} ::: {.cell-output-display} ```{=html}

In 2020, among the prison population currently eligible for parole, males constituted the majority, representing 98 percent.


``` ::: ::: ### Age ::: {.cell} ::: {.cell-output-display} ```{=html}

In 2020, among the prison population currently eligible for parole, people between the ages of 25-34 years constituted the majority, representing 35 percent.


``` ::: ::: ::: ::: {.cell} ::: {.cell-output-display} ```{=html}

Criminal Offenses

In 2020, among the prison population currently eligible for parole, people with rape or sexual assault offenses constituted the majority, representing 22 percent.


``` ::: ::: ::: {.cell} ::: {.cell-output-display} ```{=html}

Sentence Lengths

In 2020, among the prison population currently eligible for parole, people with sentences between 2 to 4.9 years years constituted the majority, representing 35 percent.



``` ::: ::: ::: {.cell} ::: {.cell-output-display} ```{=html}

Missing Data

This section will have information about missing parole eligibility data for the state once new NCRP data is released.

``` ::: :::

## Prison Releases
::: {.cell} ::: {.cell-output-display} ```{=html}

Prison Release Trends

Sentence about change in releases from 2010 to 2020.



This chart shows an annual breakdown from 2010 to 2020 on the proportion of the parole-eligible population that was released each year compared to those who remained incarcerated despite their parole eligibility.



``` ::: ::: {.cell-output-display} ```{=html}

Prison Release Types

Sentence about conditional and unconditional releases.




``` ::: ::: ::: {.cell} ::: {.cell-output-display} ```{=html}

``` ::: ::: ### Age ::: {.cell} ::: {.cell-output-display} ```{=html}

Sentence about age at release.



``` ::: ::: ::: ::: {.cell} ::: {.cell-output-display} ```{=html}

Timing of Release

This chart illustrates the timing of releases in relation to parole eligibility year by release type in 2020, comparing conditional and unconditional releases. It shows the proportion of individuals released before their parole eligibility year, during the year they were eligible, within one to five years after eligibility, more than five years after, and those with missing parole eligibility data.



This graphic depicts the distribution of release timings for different types of offenses in 2020. It shows the percentages of individuals released at various intervals relative to their parole eligibility year, ranging from those released before their parole eligibility year to those who remained incarcerated more than five years after becoming eligible for parole.



``` ::: ::: ::: {.cell} ::: {.cell-output-display} ```{=html}
